food_name: Fried Pork Sausage (Doi Sun Chien Gion)
region_of_origin: Vietnam / Singapore (Vietnamese-style dish served in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pork sausage food product class. No specific entry for Vietnamese-style fried pork sausage (Doi Sun Chien Gion). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers pork sausages generically. No specific code for deep-fried Vietnamese-style pork sausage. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific fried pork sausage preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Fried Pork Sausage (Doi Sun Chien Gion) consists of 2 to 3 pieces totalling approximately 120 g, as served at Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pork sausage, cooked (USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy, NDB No. 10165)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact match for Vietnamese-style Doi Sun Chien Gion in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork sausage (NDB 10165 / FDC ID 174608), adjusted upward for fat and energy to reflect deep-frying in oil. Protein, carbohydrate, and sodium values retained from USDA base. This is the closest defensible approved-source anchor available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Vietnamese-style fried pork sausage (Doi Sun Chien Gion). GI estimated at 28 based on the analogous entry for pork sausage in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI ~28, low category), consistent with high-fat, low-carbohydrate processed meat products. Glycemic load per 120 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (28 × 3.36) / 100 ≈ 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (748 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this dish due to its high sodium content (~898 mg per 120 g serving), which represents approximately 39% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; portion restriction and avoidance of additional salty condiments is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High saturated fat and moderate energy density despite low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the glycemic index of fried pork sausage is low (~28), its high saturated fat content (10.2 g per 100 g) and energy density (339 kcal per 100 g) may contribute to insulin resistance and weight gain in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; consumption should be occasional and portion-controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat, high sodium, and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fried pork sausage is high in saturated fat (10.2 g/100 g), sodium (748 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (72 mg/100 g), all of which are established risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with existing cardiovascular conditions should minimise intake and prefer leaner, lower-sodium protein sources.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Pork Sausage, known in Vietnamese as Dồi Sụn Chiên Giòn (romanized: Doi Sun Chien Gion), is a Vietnamese-style deep-fried pork sausage dish served at Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore, including establishments such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant. The sausage is typically made from a mixture of ground pork, pork fat, and pork cartilage (sụn), seasoned with garlic, lemongrass, fish sauce, and black pepper, then stuffed into a casing and deep-fried until the exterior is golden and crispy while the interior remains juicy and tender. The name 'Doi Sun' refers to the cartilage-enriched sausage, and 'Chien Gion' means 'fried crispy' in Vietnamese. The dish is characterised by a crunchy outer skin, a savoury and slightly herbal flavour profile, and a chewy texture from the cartilage. It is typically served as a starter or side dish, often accompanied by fresh herbs, pickled vegetables, and dipping sauces such as fish sauce with chilli and lime. Nutritionally, this dish is energy-dense (approximately 339 kcal per 100 g) with high fat content (29.5 g/100 g), moderate protein (13.6 g/100 g), and low carbohydrate (2.8 g/100 g), resulting in a low glycemic index (~28). Its high sodium (748 mg/100 g) and saturated fat (10.2 g/100 g) content make it a food of concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or Type 2 Diabetes. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher diets.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Doi Sun Chien Gion was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or ICMR-NIN databases. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest approved-source anchor (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, cooked pork sausage), with fat and energy adjusted upward to reflect deep-frying. GI is estimated from the analogous University of Sydney GI Database entry for pork sausage. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174608, cooked pork sausage); fat_g and energy_kcal adjusted upward to reflect deep-frying in oil. No exact HPB or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry found for this specific Vietnamese-style fried pork sausage. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for cooked pork sausage; this is a meat-based product with no plant fibre. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this product category. 4. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this product category. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this product category. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this product category. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this product category. 8. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for pork sausage; no direct GI measurement for Doi Sun Chien Gion was found in peer-reviewed literature. 9. The dish is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant (Circular Road, Singapore) per GrabFood Singapore source data. 10. is_halal is false as the dish contains pork. 11. The cuisine tag is Singapore as the dish is served in Singapore, though its culinary origin is Vietnamese.
